ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for yam-filled xiao long bao. 'dumpling food product' (FOODON_03302757) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Steamed dumplings) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for yam-filled xiao long bao variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for yam xiao long bao or steamed dumpling at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 10 steamed yam xiao long bao pieces, weighing approximately 200 g in total, as served at Din Tai Fung Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Taro (cooked, without salt) and steamed wheat dumpling skin — composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for yam xiao long bao exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA databases. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked taro (FDC ID 170071) representing the yam filling (~40% by weight), and (2) USDA SR Legacy steamed wheat dumpling wrapper data (~60% by weight). Energy and macros are weighted-average composites. Sodium is estimated from typical dim sum preparation. All values are per 100 g of the combined food as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of taro and wheat-based steamed dumplings — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for taro (GI 54) and steamed wheat dumplings (GI 56)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for yam xiao long bao. GI estimated as 55 (low) based on weighted average of: (1) cooked taro GI ~54 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr 87:214S–220S) representing ~40% of food mass, and (2) steamed wheat dumpling GI ~56 (University of Sydney GI Database) representing ~60% of food mass. Weighted composite: (0.40 × 54) + (0.60 × 56) = 21.6 + 33.6 = 55.2, rounded to 55. GL per serving = (55 × 42.5 × 200) / (100 × 100) = 46.75, rounded to 46.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g; ~420 mg per 10-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 5 pieces per sitting, as a full 10-piece serving contributes approximately 420 mg sodium (~18% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should avoid adding soy sauce or chilli dipping condiments which substantially increase sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but high glycemic load per serving (~46.8) due to refined wheat wrapper and naturally sweet taro filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to 3–5 pieces per occasion and pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the full 10-piece serving delivers approximately 85 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 46.8, despite its low glycemic index.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Relatively low saturated fat (~1.4 g per 100 g) and absence of trans fat; taro provides potassium and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed yam xiao long bao is a lower-fat dim sum option with no trans fat and moderate saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume a moderate portion (5 pieces) as part of a balanced meal, while monitoring overall carbohydrate and sodium intake from the full meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Yam Xiao Long Bao (芋泥小笼包) is a dessert-style variant of the classic Shanghai steamed soup dumpling (xiao long bao), featuring a smooth, sweetened mashed taro (yam) filling encased in a thin, pleated wheat-flour wrapper folded into the signature 18-fold pattern. Unlike the savoury pork-and-broth original, this version contains no meat broth; instead, the taro filling provides a naturally sweet, creamy, and slightly starchy flavour profile with a soft, velvety texture. The dish is steamed in bamboo baskets and served hot. In Singapore, it is most closely associated with Din Tai Fung, which uses premium Thai taro for its filling. Nutritionally, each 10-piece serving (approximately 200 g) provides roughly 436 kcal, 85 g carbohydrate, 8.4 g protein, and 7.6 g fat. The low glycemic index (~55) but high glycemic load per serving (~46.8) make portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium per serving is approximately 420 mg, which is relevant for hypertension management. Taro contributes dietary fibre (~2.1 g per 100 g), potassium (~280 mg per 100 g), and vitamin B6, offering modest micronutrient value. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan (may contain lard in the wrapper at some establishments) and contains gluten. It is classified as a processed grain-based dim sum item.
common_user_queries: How many calories in yam xiao long bao?
Is taro xiao long bao healthy?
Carbs in steamed yam dumplings Din Tai Fung
GI of yam xiao long bao
Can diabetics eat yam xiao long bao?
Is yam xiao long bao vegetarian?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for steamed yam xiao long bao exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked taro and steamed wheat dumpling wrappers. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for taro and steamed wheat dumplings. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates (40% taro filling, 60% wheat wrapper by weight) derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No direct laboratory analysis of Din Tai Fung's specific recipe is available in approved sources. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: steamed wheat-and-taro dumplings contain no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: this is a plant-based filling (taro) with a wheat wrapper; no animal-derived cholesterol-containing ingredients are present in the standard recipe. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero: this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard formulation; vitamin B12 is naturally zero. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for taro or plain wheat dumpling wrappers. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for taro or plain wheat dumpling wrappers. 7. Halal status is marked false because Din Tai Fung Singapore's yam xiao long bao wrappers may contain lard; consumers should verify with the restaurant directly. 8. GI estimated as 55 (low) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries; glycemic load per serving = 46.8 based on 200 g serving and 42.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. 9. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate for 10 standard-sized xiao long bao pieces (~20 g each); actual weight may vary by restaurant. 10. The food_group is retained as 'Grains and staples' per input, consistent with the wheat-wrapper-dominant composition, though the dish functions as a dessert dim sum item.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy taro and wheat dumpling data since no direct approved-source entry exists.
Derived GI as weighted average of University of Sydney GI Database entries for taro (GI 54) and steamed wheat dumplings (GI 56).
Added all five mandatory Singaporean-cuisine languages: English (food_name), Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-absence justification in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) with confirmed-zero or naturally-zero justification phrases in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list for perfect string consistency across all language keys.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Set human_condition_name to 'Type 2 Diabetes' with machine key type2_diabetes.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, and updated all related descriptions and notes in gi_source, health_context, llm_training, and notes sections to reflect this change.
